Fuente's story, a letter to God from the insert class 10 English notes. And honestly, it's such a layered story. It really is. And listen, if you have found this chapter difficult, to grasp, or if you're feeling overwhelmed by the themes, just take a deep breath.

None at all. We've got our detailed chapter notes right here, which cover all the main events, the character analysis, and we'll even break down some of that tricky vocabulary for you. Yeah, because we really want to establish what this story is actually doing. We're looking at some huge core themes here.

The incredible power of faith, the sort of unavoidable conflict between humans and nature, which is brutal in this story. It really is. And ultimately, this very funny, but also kind of deeply tragic misunderstanding between people. Yeah, that misunderstanding is what's the kicker.

But I promise you, by the end of this deep dive, you're going to understand every single detail of this story. And we're even going to top it all off with a fun little five question quiz at the end just to make sure you got it locked in. Sounds perfect. OK, let's unpack this.

We start right off with our main guy, Lencho, and the text describes him as an ox of a man, which, you know, gives you a pretty clear picture right away. Right. He's incredibly hardworking, a rugged farmer. Exactly.

And he lives in this house, the only house in the entire valley, actually, sitting up on this low hill. And from there, he can look right down at his field of ripe corn. And the corn is dotted with flowers, which means a good harvest is basically guaranteed. Right.

But there's a catch. The earth desperately needs just one thing, rain. Just a little bit of rain. Yeah.

And to make this relatable, because, you know, maybe you aren't a corn farmer, think about it like this. Imagine you are a student, right? And you have studied all semester, you've done all the homework, and now you are just waiting for that final A grade to come through to like completely secure your future. That's a really good way to put it.

The heavy lifting is done. You're just waiting on that final confirmation. Right. You're just staring at your inbox waiting for the grade.

And for Lencho, he's just staring at this guy. He's so excited when the rain finally starts that he actually calls the raindrops new coins. He says the big drops are 10 cent pieces and the little ones are fives. Because for his family, that rain literally equals money.

It's their survival. Exactly. But then things take a really sudden turn. Yeah, they do.

And this is where the disaster hits. Because that gentle money making rain, it suddenly shifts, the wind picks up and it turns into this massive, violent hailstorm. It's just devastating. Completely, the hail falls for an hour.

The text says it looks like frozen pearls or new silver coins. And when it finally stops, the entire valley is just totally white. It says it looked as if it were covered with salt. Covered with salt.

Which I just want to jump in here and relate to that devastating feeling for a second. Because it is so sudden. It's exactly like, OK, imagine you're working on a 20 page final term paper. Oh, no, I know where this is going.

Right. You've spent weeks on it. And right before you click submit, your computer just completely crashes and deletes every single word. The absolute worst feeling in the world.

Just totally wiped out. And that is Lencho's crop. Gone. He even stands there and says a plague of locusts would have left more behind.

Because bugs would at least leave the stocks. The hail left literally nothing. He is terrified that his family is going to go hungry that year. So what does this all mean for Lencho?

I mean, when all human effort fails, when your 20 page paper is gone and there is no backup file, where does a person turn? Well, despite this absolute total ruin, Lencho's family clings to one single hope, direct help from God. They keep repeating this proverb to comfort each other. No one dies of hunger.

Which is a bold thing to say when your entire food supply was just destroyed. It is. But it shows the depth of his unwavering faith. Lencho firmly believes that God sees everything.

He believes God can even see what is deep inside a person's conscience. Oh, wait, let's pause right there. Since we're making sure you have all the tools you need for this chapter, let me just quickly define that word for you. Conscience.

Think of your conscience as that little inner voice in your head that tells you what is right and what is wrong. Right. Your moral compass. Exactly.

Right. So Lencho believes God can see his good conscience, his good intentions. And because of that, God will help him. Yes.

And here is where Lencho takes action. Remember, he's a farmer, an ox of a man, but he's also literate. He knows how to write. So the following Sunday, he sits down and he actually writes a letter.

Which is just it's wild to think about. It's like, OK, imagine you're desperate, right? It's like setting up a GoFundMe page because you desperately need financial help. But instead of like emailing the link to your friends or sharing it on social media, you somehow send the link directly to the creator of the universe.

That's exactly what he does. He makes a very specific request, too. He asks God for exactly 100 pesos. Not a million.

Just what he needs. Exactly. Just 100 pesos to sow his field again and to survive until the next crop comes in. He puts the letter in an envelope, writes to God on the outside and just drops it in the mail.

Just drops it in the mailbox. Yeah. And you know, this actually connects back to that money order activity that's in your notes. Yeah.

Because normally when you send money or a letter, you need a sender, a receiver with a real address and acknowledgment slip all these official steps. Right. The postal bureaucracy. Yeah.

But Lynch Oh completely bypasses all the normal post office rules by just making God the receiver. He just trusts it will get there. Which raises an important question, right? What happens when a very strict human institution receives a letter intended for the divine?

Well, we get to see what happens. The postman finds it while he's sorting the mail. He sees to God written on the envelope and he just starts laughing heartily. I mean, he's never seen an address like that in his life.

Of course not. So he takes it to his boss, the postmaster. Now your text describes the postmaster as a fat and amiable fellow. Let's define amiable real quick.

If someone is amiable, it just means they are a really friendly, pleasant and warm hearted person. Like a big teddy bear of a guy. Exactly. A big teddy bear.

And initially, this amiable postmaster laughs just as hard as the postman. But what's fascinating here is how quickly that changes. The postmaster has this sudden shift. He completely stops laughing.

He taps the letter on his desk and he is just deeply moved by Lynch Oh's face. He's actually inspired by it. He really is. He decides right then that he has to answer the letter.

He wants to protect Lynch Oh's face so it doesn't get shattered. But answering the letter isn't just about writing a nice note back. Lynch Oh asked for cash. Right.

A hundred pesos. Yeah. Which is a lot of money. So the postmaster starts this sort of secret mission.

He donates part of his own salary. He asks his employees to chip in whatever they can. And he even gets his friends to donate for this act of charity. It's a huge community effort.

It is. And, you know, imagine finding out a little kid at your school still genuinely believes in the tooth fairy. Oh, I love this comparison. Right.

Like instead of telling them the truth and ruining their belief, you rally your whole classroom to chip in a few coins to secretly sneak under their pillow. That's exactly the spirit of what the postmaster is doing. Yeah. But even with all that effort, they can't raise the full 100.

They managed to get a little over half 70 pesos. So the postmaster puts the cash in an envelope, addresses it to Lencho and simply signs it God. Wow. OK, so this is where it gets really interesting because you would think the story ends there.

Everyone is happy. You would think so. But no good deed goes unpunished. Tell us about Lencho's reaction when he gets the letter.

So the next Sunday, Lencho comes to the post office, the postman hands in the envelope and Lencho shows absolutely zero surprise. None. Which is just I mean, I would be floored. Anyone would.

But his faith is so absolute that he fully expected God to mail him cash. It was a given. But then he opens it, he counts the money and he gets angry, angry, very angry because in his mind, God is perfect. God couldn't possibly make a math mistake and God certainly wouldn't deny him what he asked for.

So if there are only 70 pesos in the envelope, someone else must have messed up. Oh my gosh. So he marches right up to the desk, gets some paper and writes a second letter to God. Yeah.

He demands the rest of the money, but he explicitly writes and this is the craziest part. He writes, don't send it to me through the mail because the post office employees are a bunch of crooks. It's incredible. We really have to unpack the two major conflicts happening in the story here.

First, you have man versus nature, which is the hailstorm destroying the crops. But then you have man versus man. And the irony here is just massive. The very people who sacrificed their own hard earned money to save Lencho, the ones who acted out of pure charity, are the exact same people he accuses of robbing him.

It's almost frustrating to read, but, you know, we should clarify Lencho isn't trying to be a bad guy here, is he? No, not at all. He is incredibly naive and unquestioning. And in this specific moment, yeah, he is ironically ungrateful, but the text is clear that he is not greedy.

He only asked for exactly what he needed to survive. And he's not stupid either. He's a literate, hardworking farmer. He just has a massive blind spot because of his absolute faith.

Yeah, that blind spot is huge. It's like, OK, think about it this way. Imagine you order a pizza because you're starving, right? And your neighbor secretly pays for it as a wonderful kind surprise.

But on the way to your door, the delivery guy trips and drops one slice. So when you open the box and see a slice missing, you march over and accuse your generous neighbor of stealing a slice of your pizza completely unaware that they just bought the whole thing for you. Yes, that is the perfect analogy. He is so focused on what is missing that he completely misses the miracle of what was given to him by the people right next door.

Wow. What a brilliant, brilliant story. And honestly, if you listening right now have made it through this whole breakdown, you should be super proud of yourself. You've tackled the hardest parts of this chapter.

Absolutely. You've got all the tools you need now. Which means it is time for our promised review. We are going to do a quick five question recap quiz.

I will read a question. And if you need to just hit pause on your device, think about your answer, and then we'll tell you if you got it right. No pressure. Just a fun little check in.

Exactly. Okay, here we go. Question one. What did Lencho initially hope for?

And what did he compare the raindrops to? Pause if you need a second. The answer is he hoped for rain for his harvest and he compared the raindrops to new coins. The big ones were 10 cent pieces and the small ones were fives.

Perfect. Okay. Question two. What natural disaster completely destroyed Lencho's crops, leaving the valley looking white as salt?

Take a think. And the answer is a massive hailstorm. Yep. The frozen pearls.

All right. Question three. Who did Lencho write his letter to and exactly how much money did he ask for? Okay.

The answer is he wrote his letter directly to God asking for exactly 100 pesos. Spot on. Question four. We learned a new vocabulary word to describe the postmaster.

What does amiable mean? Remember the big kettie bear. The answer is amiable means friendly, pleasant and warm hearted. Great.

And the final question. Question five. In his second letter, why did Lencho call the post office employees a bunch of crux? The ultimate irony.

The answer is he thought they stole 30 pesos from the 100 pesos he believed God had sent him, completely not realizing they were actually the ones who gave him the 70 pesos. You did amazing if you followed along with those. Really great job mastering a letter to God. It's a tough chapter with a lot of complex emotional stuff going on, but you absolutely nailed it.

Before we go, I just want to leave you with one final thought to maybe mull over on your own. Oh, I love these. What do you get? Well, think about this.

If the postmaster had somehow managed to send the full 100 pesos, if he didn't fall 30 pesos short, would Lencho have ever learned how to survive on his own in the real world? Oh, wow. Right. Or does his extreme unquestioning naivety mean he would just forever be dependent on miracles, completely disconnected from the actual human kindness right in front of him?

That is such a fascinating question. Does getting exactly what you want actually stop you from seeing the real world? Definitely something to think about. Thank you all so much for joining us for this deep dive.

Keep that curiosity alive. Keep studying hard and we will see you next time.
